affordances.name: Reversal-cost dependency audit
affordances.mechanism: When a platform, vendor, architecture, or migration decision is framed as reversible, require the answer to test whether integration debt, dual-run burden, retraining, contract sequencing, and local workarounds are quietly narrowing the future option set.

use_when: A vendor exit, platform shift, ERP migration, or infrastructure decision is being framed as normal implementation effort.
The team says it can switch later without testing whether the option survives time.
Dual-run coexistence, retraining, workarounds, or interface debt may be the hidden cost center.
do_not_use_when: The current path is deliberately sticky because stability, standardization, or commitment is still compounding advantage.
Switching later is being priced as if it were the same option as switching now.
The old architecture or belief is being defended mainly because departure feels costly, disloyal, or identity-threatening.
case_evidence_needed: The platform, vendor, architecture, or path being locked in.
The integration debt, contract dependencies, workflows, retraining, and workaround behavior created by the current path.
The cost of dual-run coexistence and rollback.
The evidence that the option to switch later remains alive or is already narrowing.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to price the reversal and coexistence costs before accepting claims that the team can switch later.
evidence_required: The dependencies and workflows that will harden over time.
The dual-run, rollback, support, retraining, and partner costs.
The trigger at which waiting destroys the option to switch.
good_output_shape: Separates useful stability from accidental dependency hardening.
States whether the later option is still alive.

diagnostic_questions: What dependency becomes harder to unwind each month we stay on this path?
Are we pricing dual-run coexistence and local workarounds, or only target build and cutover?
Is switching later still the same option as switching now?
misuse_guards: Do not treat future switching as equally cheap after custom workflows and contracts harden.
Do not defend an old architecture mainly because departure feels disloyal or identity-threatening.
Do not ignore productive lock-in when stability and standardization are deliberately creating advantage.

source_evidence.source_quote: persistent adherence to a previous state
reversal cost compounds faster than the migration plan acknowledges
source_evidence.section_hint: Additional Application Domain: Operational and Platform Lock-In

source_evidence.source_quote: Dual-run periods, exception handling, retraining, and partner workarounds can become the mechanism that locks the organization into the old path

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 5 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports an operational lock-in audit centered on reversal cost, dual-run traps, and dependency hardening.
dropped_material: Did not extract AI persona consistency, mastery, or System 1 schema speed as separate affordances because PR32 targets platform/decision lock-in gaps.
Did not infer procurement or migration tactics beyond source support.
open_questions: Future packet comparison should test overlap between lock-in, path-dependence, commitment-bias, and status-quo-bias.

absence_records.reason: The source says switching later should not be priced as if it were the same option after workflows, contracts, and operating habits harden.

source_evidence.source_quote: switching later is priced as if it were the same option as switching now

absence_records.reason: The source rejects defending an old architecture, strategy, or belief mainly because departure feels costly, disloyal, or identity-threatening.

source_evidence.source_quote: defend an old architecture, strategy, or belief mainly because departure feels costly, disloyal, or identity-threatening
silently losing freedom while the team still talks as if freedom is intact
source_evidence.section_hint: Additional Application Domain: Operational and Platform Lock-In

source_evidence.source_quote: Most useful when a migration plan treats reversal as available even though dependency drag is already compounding

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 5 source reading found strong reviewed depth for Lock-In. Extracted one reversal-cost/dependency audit and rejected switch-later equivalence plus identity-protective defense.
dropped_material: General consistency benefits were kept as do-not-overcorrect context rather than separate PR32 cards.
No runtime, prompt, lane, packet, or user-facing change is implied.
open_questions: Later packet comparison should verify whether this card and path-dependence can coexist without duplicate bulk.
